content:
# Genesis Chapter 25 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 14r–14v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_025_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_025_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), 4th century CE — LXX | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0025 (page 21), https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0026 (page 22)

---

## The Last Days of Abraham

**1** Abraham married again. His new wife's name was Keturah. **2** She bore him six sons: Zimran, Jokshan, Medan, Midian, Ishbak, and Shuah. **3** Jokshan became the father of Sheba and Dedan, and from Dedan came the Asshurim, the Letushim, and the Leummim. **4** Midian's sons were Ephah, Epher, Hanoch, Abida, and Eldaah. All of these were Keturah's descendants.

**5** But Abraham left everything he owned to Isaac. **6** To the sons he had by his concubines he gave gifts while he was still alive, then sent them away to the east, away from his son Isaac.

---

**7** Abraham lived a hundred and seventy-five years in all. **8** Then he breathed his last — worn out and content, full of years — and he was gathered to his people. **9** His sons Isaac and Ishmael buried him in the cave of Machpelah, in the field of Ephron son of Zohar the Hittite, east of Mamre — **10** the field that Abraham had bought from the Hittites. That is where Abraham was buried, with his wife Sarah. **11** After Abraham's death, God blessed his son Isaac. And Isaac settled near Beer-lahai-roi.

---

## The Line of Ishmael

**12** This is the family line of Ishmael, Abraham's son, whom Hagar the Egyptian — Sarah's slave — had borne to Abraham.

**13** These are the names of Ishmael's sons, listed in birth order: Nebaioth, Ishmael's firstborn; then Kedar, Adbeel, Mibsam, **14** Mishma, Dumah, Massa, **15** Hadad, Tema, Jetur, Naphish, and Kedemah. **16** These were Ishmael's sons — twelve princes, each with his own settlement and camp, by name. **17** Ishmael was a hundred and thirty-seven years old when he died. He breathed his last and was gathered to his people. **18** His descendants settled from Havilah to Shur — the region east of Egypt, toward Assyria. They lived in hostility toward all their brothers.

---

## The Birth of Jacob and Esau

**19** This is the family line of Isaac, Abraham's son.

**20** Isaac was forty years old when he married Rebekah daughter of Bethuel the Aramean from Paddan-aram, the sister of Laban the Aramean. **21** Rebekah was unable to have children, so Isaac prayed to the LORD on her behalf. The LORD heard his prayer, and Rebekah became pregnant.

**22** But the babies fought each other inside her so violently that she cried out, "If it's going to be like this, why do I even go on?" And she went to ask the LORD what was happening.

**23** The LORD said to her:

*"Two nations are inside you.*
*Two peoples, even now, are pulling apart.*
*One people will be stronger than the other.*
*The older will serve the younger."*

---

**24** When the time came for her to give birth, there were twins in her womb. **25** The first one out was reddish all over, covered in hair like a fur coat. They named him Esau. **26** Then his brother came out, his fist closed tight around Esau's heel. They named him Jacob. Isaac was sixty years old when they were born.

---

## Two Brothers

**27** As the boys grew up, Esau became a skilled hunter, a man of the open country. Jacob was a quieter sort — a man who kept close to home and the tents. **28** Isaac loved Esau, because Esau's hunting kept his table stocked with game. But Rebekah loved Jacob.

---

## The Birthright

**29** One day Jacob was cooking a pot of stew when Esau came in from the fields, starving. **30** "Let me have some of that red stuff," Esau gasped. "I'm dying of hunger." (That is why he was also called Edom — Red.)

**31** "First," said Jacob, "sell me your birthright."

**32** "What good is a birthright to me when I'm about to die?" said Esau.

**33** "Swear to me first," Jacob said. So Esau swore an oath, and sold his birthright to Jacob. **34** Jacob gave him bread and lentil stew. Esau ate and drank, got up, and walked away.

That was how little Esau thought of his birthright.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### Genesis 25 — the pivot of the patriarchal story

Genesis 25 is the hinge chapter of the whole patriarchal narrative. It opens by closing: Abraham dies, Isaac inherits everything, and the lines that run parallel to the covenant — Keturah's sons, Ishmael's princes — are carefully documented and then set aside. The chapter then pivots to the generation that will dominate Genesis for the next twenty-five chapters: Jacob and Esau, born fighting, and already displaying in the womb the conflict that will shape the story of Israel.

---

### Verse 1 — Keturah, wife or concubine?

**וַיֹּסֶף אַבְרָהָם וַיִּקַּח אִשָּׁה וּשְׁמָהּ קְטוּרָה** — "Abraham took another wife, and her name was Keturah." The Hebrew calls her *ishah* ("wife") here in verse 1, but verse 6 calls her — along with the other women — a *pilegesh* ("concubine"). 1 Chronicles 1:32 explicitly labels Keturah a concubine. The tension is ancient. For the purposes of this narrative, what matters is the legal and inheritance consequence that the narrator himself draws out: Abraham gave *everything* to Isaac (v.5) and sent the other sons away with gifts. Keturah's sons are ancestors, not heirs.

---

### Verse 8 — "gathered to his people"

**וַיִּגְוַע וַיָּמָת אַבְרָהָם בְּשֵׂיבָה טוֹבָה זָקֵן וְשָׂבֵעַ וַיֵּאָסֶף אֶל-עַמָּיו** — "And Abraham breathed his last and died at a ripe old age, old and full, and was gathered to his people." Three expressions cluster together to describe the death as thoroughly good: *seyva tova* ("good old age" / "ripe old age"), *zaqen* ("old"), *saveya* ("sated/full/content"). The Hebrew is emphatic in its contentment. The life that began in a migrant's tent in Ur ends with an old man who has had enough — who is *sated* — and who departs satisfied. I have rendered this "worn out and content, full of years" to catch all three notes.

The phrase **ne'esaf el-'ammav** — "gathered to his people" — is one of the most evocative death idioms in the Hebrew Bible. It does not describe burial, since Abraham's burial is only mentioned afterward. It points toward something else: a joining, a returning, a gathering to those who have gone before. The phrase is used consistently for the patriarchs (Isaac in 35:29, Jacob in 49:33) and for Moses (Num. 27:13). It is not a fully developed afterlife theology, but it is not nothing either. The Hebrew imagination holds the dead as gathered, present somewhere, a people.

---

### Verse 11 — Beer-lahai-roi again

**וַיְהִי אַחֲרֵי מוֹת אַבְרָהָם וַיְבָרֶךְ אֱלֹהִים אֶת-יִצְחָק בְּנוֹ וַיֵּשֶׁב יִצְחָק עִם-בְּאֵר לַחַי רֹאִי** — "After Abraham's death, God blessed his son Isaac. And Isaac settled near Beer-lahai-roi." The inheritance passes. God blessed Abraham; now the same God blesses Isaac. The blessing is the continuity of the covenant, not merely material wealth. And Isaac returns to Beer-lahai-roi — the well of Hagar, the well where God saw the outcast woman and promised her a future. Isaac is drawn to this place. He lived there before Rebekah came (Genesis 24:62). He lives there again after his father dies. The reader notices but is not told why.

---

### Verse 18 — "fell in hostility"

**עַל-פְּנֵי כָל-אֶחָיו נָפָל** — "Over against all his brothers he fell/settled." The verb *naphal* ("fell") is the same verb used of Ishmael in Genesis 16:12 — *yado vakkol ve-yad kol bo, ve-al-peney kol-echav yishkon* ("his hand against everyone and everyone's hand against him, and in the face of all his brothers he shall dwell"). There the LXX renders it *katoikei* (he shall dwell). Here the Hebrew switches to the perfect *naphal* — literally "fell." The traditional translation oscillates between "settled" and "fell upon," generating long debate. The LXX here reads **κατῴκησεν** (he dwelt/settled), harmonizing with 16:12. The ambiguity is real: *naphal* can mean to settle/fall into a region, but it can also mean to fall upon in hostility. The manifest notes this as a genuine crux. I have rendered it as "lived in hostility" — which honors both the genealogical note (they were *there*, in that region) and the tone established by 16:12.

---

### Verse 21 — "the LORD heard his prayer"

**וַיֶּעְתַּר יִצְחָק לַיהוָה לְנֹכַח אִשְׁתּוֹ כִּי עֲקָרָה הִוא וַיֵּעָתֶר לוֹ יְהוָה** — "And Isaac entreated the LORD opposite his wife because she was barren, and the LORD was entreated by him." The verb **עָתַר** (*'atar*) in the qal means to pray/entreat; in the niphal it means to be prevailed upon, to be moved by the prayer, to answer it. It is rare and strong — not the ordinary word for prayer. The Niphal form here makes the LORD the object of the entreating — he *allows himself to be moved*. This is one of the few places in Genesis where a patriarch intercedes for his wife explicitly, and God explicitly responds. I rendered it "Isaac prayed to the LORD on her behalf. The LORD heard his prayer" — capturing both the act and the answer without losing the relational movement of the verbs.

---

### Verse 22 — *vayitrotsatsu*

**וַיִּתְרֹצְצוּ הַיְלָדִים בְּקִרְבָּהּ** — "And the children shoved/crushed each other within her." This is the Hithpoel of **רָצַץ** (*ratsats*) — to crush, to shatter, to violently press against. The Hithpoel makes it reciprocal: they were crushing each other. This is not gentle movement or kicking; it is described as violent mutual assault. The word is used elsewhere for the oppression of the weak by the powerful. Rebekah's pregnancy is agonizing and alarming enough that she cries out to God — "if it's like this, why am I even here?" I rendered *lamah zeh anokhi* ("why am I this?") as "why do I even go on?" — her distress is existential, not merely physical. She is not asking a medical question; she is asking a meaning question.

---

### Verse 23 — the oracle: which is older, which is younger?

**רַב יַעֲבֹד צָעִיר** — "Rab ya'avod tsa'ir" is one of the most discussed syntactic puzzles in Genesis. It consists of only three words: *rab* (great/elder), *ya'avod* (will serve), *tsa'ir* (lesser/younger). Hebrew does not mark subject and object by word order the way English does; both nouns are in the absolute state, and either can be the subject. So the phrase can mean either "the elder shall serve the younger" or "the elder, the younger shall serve" — i.e., the exact same two words support opposite meanings. The reader who comes to the text cold cannot resolve it from grammar alone.

Only the story itself resolves the ambiguity — and the story takes a very long time to do so. In the immediate narrative, Jacob supplants Esau. In the historical background, Israel (Jacob) occasionally dominates Edom (Esau). But there are long stretches where the opposite is true. The oracle is given in a poetic form that refuses easy determinism. I have translated it as "The older will serve the younger" — which is the reading the New Testament (Romans 9:12) and most of the tradition have followed — while flagging the ambiguity in this note.

The LXX renders it **ὁ μείζων δουλεύσει τῷ ἐλάσσονι** — "the greater shall serve the lesser" — which removes the ambiguity and fixes the direction: the elder serves the younger. The LXX translators made an interpretive choice the Hebrew withholds.

---

### Verse 25 — Esau's name

**וַיֵּצֵא הָרִאשׁוֹן אַדְמוֹנִי כֻּלּוֹ כְּאַדֶּרֶת שֵׂעָר וַיִּקְרְאוּ שְׁמוֹ עֵשָׂו** — "And the first came out reddish all over, like a hairy mantle, and they called his name Esau." Two separate wordplays are compressed here. **אַדְמוֹנִי** (*admoni*, "reddish/ruddy") anticipates *Edom* (v.30). **שֵׂעָר** (*se'ar*, "hair/hairy") anticipates the land of Seir (Esau's territory, cf. Gen. 32:3, 36:8). Esau's birth is simultaneously a description of a child and a foreshadowing of a nation. The name *Esau* itself is of uncertain etymology — possibly related to a root meaning "to do/make" or possibly onomatopoeic for the hairy texture. The birth narrative encodes in one verse the entire future of the Edomite people.

---

### Verse 26 — Jacob the heel-grasper

**וְאַחֲרֵי-כֵן יָצָא אָחִיו וְיָדוֹ אֹחֶזֶת בַּעֲקֵב עֵשָׂו וַיִּקְרָא שְׁמוֹ יַעֲקֹב** — "And after him came out his brother with his hand grasping the heel of Esau, and they called his name Jacob." **יַעֲקֹב** (*Ya'aqov*, Jacob) is from **עָקֵב** (*'aqev*, heel) — making Jacob literally "he who grabs the heel." But *'aqev* also develops a secondary meaning: to trip, to deceive, to get behind someone, to circumvent. Later in the story, Esau will use this very etymology as an accusation: "Is he not rightly named Jacob? For he has cheated me (*ya'aqveni*) these two times" (27:36). The name carries its own prophecy. Jacob is a heel-grasper from the moment of birth.

---

### Verse 27 — *tam*

**וְיַעֲקֹב אִישׁ תָּם יֹשֵׁב אֹהָלִים** — "And Jacob was a blameless/quiet/complete man, dwelling in tents." The adjective **תָּם** (*tam*) is deceptively simple and enormously loaded. It is the same word used to describe Job: **תָּם וְיָשָׁר** ("blameless and upright," Job 1:1). It is used of Noah (Gen. 6:9). It suggests wholeness, integrity, moral completeness. And yet it is used here of a man who will spend the next twenty chapters deceiving his father, stealing his brother's blessing, outwitting his father-in-law, and wrestling God to a draw. The narrator knows the irony. So does the careful reader. The contrast with Esau is not simply introvert/extrovert or domestic/adventurous; it is a difference of character that the narrator names quietly but clearly. Jacob is *tam* — whole, settled, morally oriented. Esau is a man of the outdoors, skilled and vigorous, but that is all the narrator says about him.

I have rendered *tam* as "quieter sort" — trying to catch the settled, inward quality without fully flattening the moral resonance. A fuller rendering would be "a man of integrity," but that would over-interpret at this stage of the narrative.

---

### Verse 28 — Isaac loved Esau because of the game

**וַיֶּאֱהַב יִצְחָק אֶת-עֵשָׂו כִּי-צַיִד בְּפִיו** — "And Isaac loved Esau because there was game in his mouth." This is one of the most understated and most devastating parenthetical clauses in the patriarchal narrative. Isaac's love for his firstborn son is conditional — and the condition is meat. *Tsayid be-fiv* literally means "his [Esau's] game [was] in his [Isaac's] mouth" — Isaac loved Esau because Esau fed him what he loved. The narrator does not editorialize. He does not call Isaac shallow. He simply gives the reason, and the reason is enough. The contrast with "Rebekah loved Jacob" — no reason given — makes Isaac's preference look even more transactional.

This single clause predicts the entirety of Genesis 27, where the loss of Esau's birthright blessing turns on a meal.

---

### Verse 29-34 — the birthright sale: the drama

The birthright sale (vv.29-34) is the pivot on which the Jacob-Esau story turns — and the narrator writes it with maximum dramatic economy. Esau comes in from the fields. He is famished. Jacob is cooking stew. The smell of the stew and the urgency of Esau's hunger converge in a single moment that Esau handles catastrophically.

**הַלְעִיטֵנִי נָא** (v.30) — "let me swallow some of that" — is a verb used elsewhere for force-feeding animals. Esau is not asking for a civilized meal. He is asking to be *stuffed*. The narrator then inserts the Edom etymology as if to say: this moment, this animal appetite for red stew, this is the origin of that name, that nation.

Jacob's response is immediate and cold: **מִכְרָה כַיּוֹם אֶת-בְּכֹרָתְךָ לִי** — "Sell me today your birthright." He has been waiting for this. Or perhaps the opportunity presents itself and Jacob, who grasps heels, reaches for this one too. Either way, the price of the firstborn's entire inheritance, the double portion, the family leadership, the covenant promises — the price of all this is one pot of lentil stew.

Esau's rationale is the rationale of the person who cannot see past the next five minutes: **הִנֵּה אָנֹכִי הוֹלֵךְ לָמוּת וְלָמָּה-זֶּה לִי בְּכֹרָה** — "Look, I am going to die — what use is a birthright to me?" He is not actually dying. He is very hungry. But in the logic of acute hunger, the present appetite swallows the future entirely. He swears. He eats. He goes.

**וַיִּבֶז עֵשָׂו אֶת-הַבְּכֹרָה** — "So Esau despised his birthright." The narrator's final verdict is four words in Hebrew. Not five, not a paragraph, not an explanation. Four words, landing like a door closing. The word **בָּזָה** (*bazah*) means to hold in contempt, to treat as worthless, to despise. This is the narrator's only direct moral comment on the entire scene. He has shown us what happened without comment. Now, at the end, he tells us what to think — in four words. Esau did not merely trade his birthright. He despised it. He held it cheap.

The LXX adds here: **καὶ ἔφαγεν καὶ ἔπιεν καὶ ἀναστὰς ᾤχετο· καὶ ἐφαύλισεν Ησαυ τὴν πρωτοτοκίαν** — "and he ate and drank and rose and went away; and Esau treated the birthright as trivial." The LXX's **ἐφαύλισεν** ("treated as trivial/cheap") renders *bazah* well, though perhaps with slightly less contemptuous force than the Hebrew.

---

### The structure: two toledot in one chapter

Genesis 25 contains two of the ten great **toledot** ("these are the generations of") formulas that structure the entire book of Genesis: Ishmael's (v.12) and Isaac's (v.19). This is unusual — two toledot in one chapter. What it accomplishes is a formal parallel-and-contrast: Ishmael's line is recorded, honored with twelve names and twelve princes, and then completed. Isaac's line is begun — and it immediately erupts into conflict. The form says: Ishmael's story is finished; Isaac's story is just starting, and it starts in a womb.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 14r–14v, photographed by the West Semitic Research Project (Bruce E. Zuckerman). Genesis 25 begins in the left column of folio 14r (page 001 for this chapter), following the conclusion of Genesis 24 in the right column, and continues through folio 14v (page 002), concluding at verse 34 before Genesis 26 begins. Secondary reference: Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana, pages 21–22 (pa_0025–pa_0026; accessible at https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0025 and /0026). The Vaticanus LXX reads closely with the Hebrew throughout Genesis 25. Notable LXX variations: verse 18 — the LXX reads **κατῴκησεν** ("he dwelt/settled"), resolving the Hebrew ambiguity of *naphal* toward settlement rather than hostility; verse 23 — the LXX fixes the syntactically ambiguous oracle as "the greater shall serve the lesser," removing the Hebrew's deliberate openness; verse 34 — the LXX adds explicit narrative connectives that slightly smooth the abrupt Hebrew ending. No DSS fragments of Genesis 25:1-34 are known from 4QGen-b or other Qumran manuscripts at this location.*

---

*— PNEUMA*
